notes: European Union regulations and directives governing data protection, AI, cybersecurity, and digital services. Regulations apply directly in member states; directives require national implementation. EUR-Lex provides access to EU law. The EU AI Act became effective August 2025.

data.description: EU regulation on data protection and privacy (Regulation 2016/679). The global benchmark for data protection law.
data.notes: Effective May 2018. Applies to organizations processing EU residents' data regardless of organization location. Enforced by national Data Protection Authorities (DPAs). Maximum penalties: 4% of annual global turnover or €20M.

children.description: GDPR article reference (e.g., art-32, art-5). Articles are the operative legal provisions.

data.note: EUR-Lex full-text HTML. Per-article anchors not available via stable URL pattern. Third-party sites (gdpr-info.eu) provide per-article pages but are not official EU resources.

examples.note: Security of processing — requires appropriate technical and organizational measures
Principles relating to processing of personal data
Right to erasure ('right to be forgotten')
Data protection by design and by default

children.description: GDPR chapter or recital reference. Recitals provide interpretive context for articles.

data.note: Chapters group related articles. Recitals are non-binding interpretive guidance that precede the articles in the regulation text. Courts and DPAs frequently cite recitals to interpret article requirements.

examples.note: Recital on appropriate technical and organizational measures (supports art-25 and art-32)

data.description: World's first comprehensive AI regulation (Regulation 2024/1689). Risk-based framework classifying AI systems.
data.notes: Effective August 2025 (phased). Bans certain AI practices, sets requirements for high-risk AI systems. Establishes conformity assessment procedures.

data.note: No per-article lookup URL. EUR-Lex publishes the full regulation as a single document.

examples.note: Classification rules for high-risk AI systems
Transparency obligations for certain AI systems

children.description: AI Act annex reference (Roman numerals). Annexes define technical requirements and high-risk categories.

data.note: Annexes are integral to the AI Act's risk classification system. Annex III lists high-risk AI system areas.

examples.note: Areas of high-risk AI systems (biometrics, critical infrastructure, education, employment, etc.)

data.description: EU directive on cybersecurity requirements for essential and important entities (Directive 2022/2555).
data.notes: Expands scope beyond NIS1. Requires incident reporting within 24 hours. Applies to energy, transport, banking, health, digital infrastructure. As a directive, requires national transposition — implementation varies across member states.

data.note: No per-article lookup URL. EUR-Lex publishes the full directive as a single document.

data.description: EU regulation on digital operational resilience for financial entities (Regulation 2022/2554).
data.notes: Applies to banks, insurance, investment firms, and their ICT service providers. Effective January 2025. Establishes requirements for ICT risk management, incident reporting, digital operational resilience testing, and third-party risk management.

data.note: No per-article lookup URL. EUR-Lex publishes the full regulation as a single document.
